data.title: Alibaba recruits Google DeepMind contributor to join Qwen AI team: sources
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding has recruited a research scientist from Google DeepMind to bolster development efforts for its Qwen artificial intelligence models, in an internal restructuring that has seen the departure of previous technical lead Lin Junyang.
While no successor to Lin was announced, former Google senior staff research scientist Zhou Hao was joining Alibaba as head of post-training research, replacing Yu Bowen, who also departed this week, two sources said.
Zhou, who holds a PhD from the University of Wisconsin-Madison, was a key contributor to the US tech giant’s acclaimed proprietary AI products, including Gemini 3, AI Mode and Deep Research, according to his LinkedIn profile.
In recent months, Alibaba has accelerated efforts to monetise growing global adoption of its models. It launched its flagship AI consumer app Qwen in November, powered by the company’s biggest Qwen-Max models, which have remained proprietary. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
To streamline its commercial AI strategy, which now includes the Qwen app and AI glasses, Alibaba recently merged these different units under one organisational umbrella.
Alibaba did not respond to a request for comment.


A long-standing proponent of open-source AI development, Lin pushed Alibaba to pursue this strategy as early as 2023, two years before the practice of releasing model weights publicly – allowing users to freely download and modify them – became widespread in China’s AI industry following the rise of DeepSeek.
This early decision reaped reputational and adoption gains for Qwen and China’s AI industry more broadly, with Qwen and DeepSeek models helping Chinese models overtake their US counterparts in cumulative downloads on open-source developer platform Hugging Face for the first time last year.
Lin wrote in a social media post on early Wednesday morning that he was “stepping down. bye my beloved qwen”, without specifying the reasons for his departure or whether he was quitting Alibaba entirely.
Alibaba has repeatedly emphasised its commitment to open-source AI, most recently in January, when it said in an annual review that the approach remained a pillar of its 380 billion yuan (US$53 billion) commitment to building AI and cloud infrastructure over the next three years – a figure that could still rise.
Last month, the company released its latest flagship open-source model series, Qwen-3.5, which swiftly came to dominate download figures on Hugging Face.
On social media on Wednesday, Qwen staff members paid tribute to Lin, who completed his master’s studies in linguistics at Peking University before joining Alibaba in 2019.
As technical lead, Lin was directly involved in the development of the Qwen models, while responsibility for Alibaba’s overall AI strategy, including other models such as the Wan video-generation models, belonged to Alibaba Cloud chief technology officer Zhou Jingren.
In one of his last acts as Qwen team lead on Monday, Lin oversaw the open-source release of several versions of the Qwen-3.5 model that is capable of running on consumer devices such as mobile phones and laptops. The move elicited praise from Elon Musk.
None of the departed researchers responded to requests for comment.

data.title: Hong Kong tasked with fast-tracking Northern Metropolis, boosting integration
data.description:
Hong Kong should speed up the construction of the Northern Metropolis megaproject near the border with the mainland and the development of a commodity trading ecosystem, according to the draft outline of China’s 15th five-year plan.
Other priorities included stronger integration with national development on the economic, technological and social fronts, especially deepening cross-border connections of financial markets.
The draft, which sets China’s economic and social development targets for 2026 to 2030, was tabled on Thursday at the opening of the National People’s Congress (NPC), the country’s top legislature.
While the draft echoed the previous blueprint’s support for Hong Kong’s “eight centres” strategy, it particularly highlighted the Northern Metropolis, a megaproject transforming 30,000 hectares of land near the city’s border with the mainland into an economic powerhouse and housing hub.
“[The plan] supports Hong Kong in developing into an international innovation and technology hub, advancing its role as a centre for international legal and dispute resolution services, a regional intellectual property trading centre, and a hub for arts and cultural exchanges between China and the rest of the world, while accelerating the development of the metropolis,” the draft read.
The draft also backed the city’s efforts to consolidate its role as an international centre for finance, trade and shipping, and aviation.
It added new tasks for Hong Kong, including the creation of a commodity trading ecosystem and a high-quality supply chain service centre. The city should also continue to strengthen its role as a global offshore renminbi business hub, and as a centre for international asset and wealth management, as well as risk management.
It also supported the city’s development into a global hub for high-end talent.

The latest blueprint was unveiled amid rising geopolitical tensions sparked by US-Israel strikes on Iran and new US global import tariffs. It also cast a shadow over President Donald Trump’s planned three-day visit to Beijing on March 31.
In supporting Hong Kong’s integration into national development, the draft prioritised strengthening the city’s cooperation with mainland China in economic, trade, technological and social areas, as well as improving measures to help Hongkongers develop and live there.
The draft also said Beijing would enhance connectivity between Hong Kong and mainland financial markets and deepen innovation synergy among industry, academia and research sectors.
Hong Kong was also tasked to play a bigger role in national policies.
“[We will] enhance mechanisms for Hong Kong and Macau to play a greater role in the country’s opening-up, support their deep participation in the high-quality development of the Belt and Road Initiative, and leverage their professional service advantages to assist enterprises in ‘going out’,” the draft read.
Key infrastructure developments were also mentioned, such as advancing the preliminary work for the Hong Kong-Shenzhen Western Rail Link and developing the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone.
The 14th five-year plan, covering 2021-2025, outlined support for Hong Kong’s “eight centres” strategy, including strengthening its role as an international centre for finance, innovation and technology, trade and shipping, and aviation.
It also positioned Hong Kong as a regional hub for international legal and dispute resolution services, as well as a centre for intellectual property (IP) trading and cultural exchange.
The plan also emphasised Hong Kong’s integration into national development, the high-quality development of the Greater Bay Area and the advancement of the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu announced in February that he would lead a task force to align Hong Kong with the national 15th five-year plan and craft the city’s own five-year development framework.
Frank Chan Fan, an NPC deputy and former Hong Kong minister for transport and housing, said the city had to study the five-year plan in depth to find ways to contribute from their respective roles.
“Our contribution should not focus only on ourselves, but more importantly on the overall development of the country,” Chan said.
Chan also noted that the city continued to hold a prominent global position in international finance, arbitration and shipping.
The deputy added that the nation had sustained strong economic growth of 5 to 6 per cent for years, and maintained a prominent global position in diplomacy and trade.
He noted that global economic conditions were influenced by multiple factors, including tariffs.

data.title: China’s state-led venture capital revival risks losing momentum
data.description:
The momentum in China’s venture-capital industry, which is recovering after years of decline, is likely to continue this year, mainly bolstered by government financing, but an expert warned that the lack of funding diversity poses a hidden risk.
Fundraising picked up in 2025, with most of the gains coming from government sources, as central policymakers loosened rules to allow local governments to issue more bonds to finance government guidance funds, according to a report released on Tuesday by Gavekal Dragonomics, a Hong Kong-based research firm.
The report said government funding was likely to rise further in the coming months following the formation of four funds.
In late December, the National Development and Reform Commission launched a 100 billion yuan (US$14.5 billion) national venture capital guidance fund, financed by ultra-long special treasury bonds.
Three regional funds have also been established, each with more than 50 billion yuan, alongside 49 sub-funds and 27 direct investment projects. The three regional funds are for the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region, the Yangtze River Delta and the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay Area.

“The funding structure is fundamentally different from previous cycles, with government and state-owned capital as the absolute driving force,” said Jin Zhao, assistant professor of finance at Cheung Kong Graduate School of Business. “The national fund’s significance lies not only in its size but also in its [impact], conveying the central government’s clear support for the venture capital industry.”
He added that the recovery was genuine, but it was largely policy-driven rather than market-driven. “A lack of funding diversity poses a hidden risk and heavy concentration of funding sources could cause the industry to stall again if fiscal policy is adjusted,” Jin said.
Jin said the sustainability of the recovery in 2026 depended on two key factors: further registration-based initial public offering (IPO) reforms to ease exit bottlenecks and a substantial return of market-oriented capital such as insurance, social security and US dollar funds.
In the first 11 months of 2025, 4,871 new venture capital funds were established in China, up 16.7 per cent from a year earlier, according to CV Source, a financial data platform affiliated to Shanghai-based CV Info. The total capital raised reached 2.29 trillion yuan, up 8.1 per cent from a year earlier.
The latest 15th five-year plan, which is set to be adopted at the “two sessions” on Thursday, was almost certain to continue prioritising industrial-policy goals, according to Gavekal Dragonomics. The plan could probably include high-level calls for more state financing for small technology companies and further development of technology financing, it added.
Besides government financing, the recovery had also drawn support from various market participants, supported by easier exit opportunities amid more IPOs and improved investor sentiment, the report said. This optimism was fuelled by technological advances from Chinese tech start-ups such as artificial intelligence firm DeepSeek and humanoid robot maker Unitree Robotics, the report added.
Despite global trade and geopolitical uncertainties, as well as a shortage of high-quality assets, several positive factors would sustain growth in China’s M&A market this year, further boosting the exit routes for venture capital, according to a report from PwC in February.
Accelerated industrial upgrades, an expected easing of refinancing conditions for leading A-share companies and deeper progress in state-owned enterprise reform and public companies’ consolidation would spur more intra-industry integration transactions, PwC said.
“State-owned capital has a relatively low risk tolerance, which may lead to capital concentrating in mid to late stage projects rather than genuine early-stage innovation,” Jin said.

data.title: Leverage unique strengths and global role, Beijing’s No 2 tells Hong Kong
data.description:
Chinese Premier Li Qiang has called on Hong Kong to leverage its “unique strengths” and pivotal role in connecting with the world, with backing from the mainland.
In the government work report he delivered at the opening session of the National People’s Congress (NPC) in Beijing on Thursday, the country’s No 2 official also stressed the need to transform the Greater Bay Area into an international innovation and technology (I&T) hub, urging the city to enhance governance efficiency.
“[We should] support their integration and contribution to the overall national development and leverage the unique advantages and important roles of Hong Kong and Macau as they are backed by the motherland and connected to the world, thus fostering long-term prosperity and stability in Hong Kong and Macau,” he said.
While the 2025 report had called on Hong Kong to uphold the constitutional order, and deepen international exchanges and cooperation, this year marked the first time Li called on the city to use its unique strengths and connections to the world, with the country’s support. Li assumed office in 2023.
NPC is China’s highest organ of state power and a legislative body that formally approves decisions made by the Communist Party. The institution, which meets annually for the “two sessions”, functions to set national economic targets and implement legal frameworks.
Li told the NPC session that the central government had stepped up support for I&T innovation, including efforts to develop the bay area into a world-class incubator for technology.
“[The central government will] promote the integration of technological innovation with industrial innovation, build Beijing, Shanghai and the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay Area into international innovation and technology hubs, and create a world-class incubator for I&T,” Li said.
Highlighting Beijing’s efforts to advance national I&T development, Li said the central government had backed the construction of pilot-scale testing platforms and strengthened intellectual property (IP) protection for emerging industries
Li also pledged support for the bay area to emerge as a “world-class” city cluster.
The report placed greater emphasis on the bay area’s I&T role than last year, when Li said the central government would boost the region’s innovation capacity.
In the section addressing Hong Kong and Macau, Li called for enhancing governance efficiency in accordance with the law, while ensuring governance by patriots.
Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office, urged the city to improve its executive-led governance model in January.
Li stressed that Hong Kong should promote its economic and social development while fully leveraging its unique advantages and pivotal role in connecting with the world.
Li said Beijing would continue supporting Hong Kong’s deeper integration into and greater contribution to national development.

Last year, he encouraged Hong Kong to deepen international exchanges and cooperation, while growing its economy and improving people’s livelihoods.
The 2025 work report further underscored the need to faithfully safeguard the constitutional order stipulated in the country’s constitution.
During Li’s presentation, he laid out major national economic targets for 2026, including GDP growth of 4.5-5 per cent, a surveyed urban unemployment rate of around 5.5 per cent, more than 12 million new urban jobs and a Consumer Price Index (CPI) increase of about 2 per cent.
But he added that the government would allow flexibility for reaching these goals.
Separately, a law report on the formulation of the national 15th five-year plan urged Hong Kong to take proactive steps to align with it.
For the first time, it outlined how Hong Kong could enhance its role in national development through proactive alignment with the country’s development plan. The latest blueprint covers the period from 2026 to 2030.
This also means that Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s proposal to draft Hong Kong’s first five-year development plan is set to become a regular practice.
Separately, the National Development and Reform Commission’s report on the nation’s economic and social development in 2025 and outlook for 2026 emphasised the need to strengthen cooperation between Hong Kong and the mainland in the areas of the economy, trade, technology and culture, while refining policies to facilitate the daily lives of Hong Kong residents on the mainland.
The report underlined the importance of consolidating Hong Kong’s status as an international financial, shipping and trade centre, and supporting its development into a global innovation and technology hub.
After attending the opening session of the National People’s Congress, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu wrote in a social media post that he would proactively align Hong Kong with the forthcoming 15th five-year plan through a systematic policy framework.
“Hong Kong’s five-year plan will adopt a macro-perspective, be strategic and forward-looking, and provide more comprehensive, precise and detailed strategic deployment for key development areas,” Lee said.
He pledged to accelerate the formulation of the plan, which he said would offer a clear roadmap and timeline for the city’s development over the next five years.
Jonathan Choi Koon-shum, a standing committee member of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference and chairman of the Chinese General Chamber of Commerce in the city, said Beijing expected Hong Kong not only to integrate into national development but also to maintain and deepen its global ties.
Choi identified Hong Kong’s adherence to international standards as a core strength, particularly in high-end service industries such as ship sales and purchase, and insurance.
Referring to recent conflicts in the Middle East, he stressed the need for market diversification and flexibility, and suggested that businesses adopted a cautious, wait-and-see approach while exploring emerging opportunities in markets such as Central Asia.
NPC deputy Rock Chen Chung-nin described Hong Kong’s key advantages over mainland cities as the free flow of capital and its common law system, calling the city “the nation’s genuine international financial centre”.
The Airport Authority board member added that Hong Kong International Airport handled the most long-haul flights in the region, urging the city to make full use of its three-runway system and new second terminal — scheduled to be fully operational in May — to boost passenger traffic and strengthen its global connectivity.

data.title: Alibaba recruits Google DeepMind contributor to join Qwen AI team: sources
data.description:

data.title: Hong Kong unveils incentives to attract gold refiners and boost global hub plan
data.description:
Hong Kong will roll out targeted measures to attract precious metals companies as part of broader efforts to establish itself as a premier global gold trading hub, according to the city’s agency tasked with attracting foreign direct investment.
InvestHK will assist companies setting up headquarters in Hong Kong with site selection and applications for overseas certifications to enhance refining operations, Benjamin Wong Kwok-fan, the agency’s head of transport, logistics and industries, said in an interview earlier this week.
After reaching an agreement in January with the Shanghai Gold Exchange and Shenzhen’s financial regulatory bureau, Hong Kong authorities will work with these parties to support trade between the city’s gold merchants and qualified refiners across the border.
“The purpose is to assist those setting up refineries in Hong Kong that haven’t yet obtained LBMA certification, offering a channel to keep operating. Other gold merchants might see this as usable too,” Wong said.
The London Bullion Market Association (LBMA) is the world’s authority for precious metals and sets standards for how they are refined.
Wong outlined ambitions for a complete value chain in Hong Kong, encompassing trading, refining, storage, exchange-traded funds, derivatives, blockchain-tokenised gold and crypto-linked products.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po has raised the city’s ambitions to be a global gold trading hub by offering tax concessions to spur bullion trading.
A key player in the initiative is Shenzhen Point Gold Refining Company, which plans to invest in Hong Kong.
“Besides Point Gold, Switzerland’s MKS PAMP, one of the leading global precious metals consortiums, has set up its regional headquarters here. There are many more discussions across storage, trading and other areas, including insurance,” Wong said.
Point Gold chairman Huang Wenbin said the company planned to invest around US$150 million in a new refining facility in Hong Kong. The firm has leased an 8,000-square-metre (86,111 sq ft) site in an industrial estate in Tai Po.
The company, already certified by LBMA and the Shanghai Gold Exchange at its Shenzhen production base – which recently secured national green factory status – must obtain separate accreditation for its Hong Kong operations.
“LBMA and Shanghai Gold Exchange certifications are required for each plant, so our Hong Kong facility needs to reapply for LBMA, LPPM [London Platinum and Palladium Market] and other qualifications. The process is complex, time-consuming and costly, requiring a period to complete,” Huang said.

Operations currently involve sourcing gold in Hong Kong, processing it in Shenzhen via a “come-and-go” or processing trade model, and re-exporting the finished products.
“All our businesses are already operating normally, but the produced gold bars are taken back to Shenzhen for processing before export,” Huang added.
He highlighted the company’s patented purification technology, which significantly reduces environmental impact and positions it for efficiency-based competition rather than price wars.
“We don’t want to viciously compete on prices with others. We only hope to enhance service and efficiency, without disrupting the existing market so everyone can make money,” he said.
The latest budget proposed tax incentives for qualified gold trading and settlement entities, in addition to earlier plans to expand Hong Kong’s gold storage capacity. It targets a capacity exceeding 2,000 tonnes within three years to establish the city as a trusted global vault.
Anderson Cheung, head of global commodities at Best Profit Capital, praised the sequenced strategy – prioritising clearing and storage before exchanges – to ensure physical delivery backing.
“Central banks continue adding gold, and retail sentiment plus institutional allocations provide strong support. Conservatively, gold could reach US$6,000 per ounce in 2026; with positive factors, even US$6,500 isn’t out of reach,” he said.

data.title: Smart meters available to Hong Kong subdivided flats meeting new living standards
data.description:
Landlords or operators of subdivided flats in Hong Kong that meet new government living standards can apply to CLP Power for smart meter installation.
The smart meters, which now cover 90 per cent of residential households supplied by CLP Power, are open for application following the Basic Housing Units Ordinance taking effect on March 1. CLP, the city’s largest power supplier, provides electricity to Kowloon and the New Territories, including Lantau and Cheung Chau.
Equipped with advanced communication systems, the meters can automatically send usage data to help customers monitor their electricity consumption. This enables users to better manage energy use and reduce costs, while also allowing earlier detection of electricity shortages so that staff can respond quickly and maintain a stable power supply.
“We will explore more artificial intelligence solutions to continue to enhance our smart meters and provide Hong Kong, and our clients, safer, more reliable, more efficient electricity supply,” said Anthony Lo Chi-wah, director of customer success and sales.
“For the basic housing units, we will also install smart meters inside, as most of our clients basically now use smart meters.”
The company began rolling out its smart meter replacement programme in late 2018. It now serves 2.8 million residential households and small to medium-sized enterprises.
The ordinance marked the beginning of Hong Kong’s regulatory push to eliminate substandard flats, uphold basic living standards and ensure buildings comply with statutory safety codes.
As of Tuesday, the Housing Bureau had received about 340 subdivided flat registration applications covering 110 building units.

The ordinance requires each basic housing unit to have a separate electricity meter to prevent landlords from overcharging tenants, ensuring they pay only for what they use.
Lo said applications for the installation of individual electricity meters were now open on the company’s official website, although there was currently no data on the number received.
The citywide roll-out of smart meters also aims to enable customers to better manage their energy use while supporting Hong Kong’s goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2050.
The meters are designed to send data to the CLP One smartphone app, allowing users to track their hourly energy consumption and estimate electricity charges.
Customers can view their hourly electricity use in the app, with data available up to three hours earlier, and receive alerts when consumption is unusually high. Lo said the feature helped users manage energy use more efficiently and cut electricity costs.
Last summer, 1.6 million households with smart meters took part in a CLP Power initiative to reduce electricity consumption over six hours on three separate occasions.
Nearly 80 per cent of participating households successfully reduced their usage, cutting around 230 tonnes of carbon emissions and saving more than 600,000 kilowatt-hours of electricity in total.
According to the company’s 2025 data, 600,000kWh is equivalent to the amount of electricity used by about 1,800 households in a month.
“During summer, when electricity usage is high, we will encourage customers to reduce the use of washing machines, raise the air-conditioning temperature or even use fans instead,” Lo said.
He said the upgraded communication systems in the smart meters allowed remote readings, enabling CLP staff to detect and respond more quickly to irregularities, such as those caused by extreme weather.

data.title: Relief, joy as first flight back from Dubai brings stranded Hongkongers home
data.description:
Hong Kong travellers rejoiced on Wednesday evening after arriving home safely on the first flight from Dubai to the city since US-Israeli strikes on Iran disrupted global air links.
Emirates flight EK380 was the first to arrive after Dubai’s two airports resumed “limited” operations two days ago, landing at around 9.50pm.
Dubai International Airport and Al Maktoum International Airport were both closed on Saturday after the conflict broke out, with Iran retaliating by targeting US military bases in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and other Gulf countries.
Among the passengers on the Airbus A380 were jockey Karis Teetan and his family members.
“Our flight was cancelled a couple of times, and we had to rebook a few times. Luckily this flight was on schedule and we are just happy to be here again,” he said.

Teetan added he could hear the missiles flying through the air in Dubai and all he could do was remain in the hotel and avoid outdoor activities.
“We were very stressed … we were just trying to be positive and not listen to what was going on.”
Twelve members and a guide from one of two Wing On Travel tour groups to Dubai were also on the seven-hour flight.
A member, surnamed Kwok, said she was glad to be back home. “I am very happy as I am finally somewhere safe, so I do not have to worry all day,” she said.
Kwok said that while the area where her hotel was located was not much affected by Iran’s missiles, she was still a little worried. “You never know where the missiles will head next.”
Another 22 tourists and a guide from the other group were waiting to fly home on Thursday if their flight was not cancelled, a Wing On Travel spokeswoman said.
Alvin Leung, who operates a restaurant in Dubai, was stuck there for four days.
“There were noticeably fewer people and cars on the street, and it was just like Hong Kong back during the Covid-19 pandemic,” he said.
Leung said Hong Kong’s Immigration Department could not offer much help.
“All we could do was register with the department,” he said. “And all they could say was ‘good luck’.”


Some family members of passengers had waited at Hong Kong International Airport’s arrival hall for the flight to land.
“We seldom pick up family at the airport but we are so happy that it feels like she has been reborn,” said retiree Nelson Yu, waiting for his sister-in-law with his wife.
Yu, 68, said his sister-in-law, who only planned to stop in Dubai on her trip to the United Kingdom, had been stranded there for three days.
He also complained that the Hong Kong authorities were not very helpful.
“We reached out to the Immigration Department’s hotline. But they said, at this moment, there was nothing they could help with except registration,” he said.
The Hong Kong women’s football team, which earlier withdrew from the Pink Ladies Cup tournament in Dubai, safely arrived in Bangkok on Wednesday, and were expected to return home on Saturday.
As of Wednesday, the Immigration Department had received inquiries from around 680 residents stranded in the Middle East, with nearly 90 per cent of them staying in the UAE. Around 100 of them had since left the region, while the rest reported that they were safe.
The government said it would maintain close communication with various airlines and urge them to help residents leave the region as soon as operational conditions in the Middle East permitted.
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways said earlier that all flights to and from Dubai in the UAE and Riyadh in Saudi Arabia had been cancelled until March 14.
Emirates said it would continue to operate a “limited flight schedule” to and from Dubai and prioritise accommodating customers with earlier bookings.

data.title: Hong Kong woman, 30, arrested over illegal sale of slimming injection
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have arrested a 30-year-old woman on suspicion of illegally selling poisons and unregistered pharmaceutical products, after an undercover operation found her offering weight-loss injections online.
The Department of Health said on Wednesday that it acted on a tip-off and obtained the anti-obesity medicine through a transaction on an instant messaging app.
The product bore a Japanese label and indicated that it contained tirzepatide.
According to a photo released by the department, the seized item was a Mounjaro injection pen manufactured by US pharmaceutical company Eli Lilly, but labelled in Japanese.
Tirzepatide is used for treating obesity and is classified as a Part 1 poison under the Pharmacy and Poisons Ordinance.
Medicines containing the substance must only be used under a doctor’s supervision and dispensed by a registered pharmacist upon prescription.
A Department of Health spokesman said the product was suspected to be an unregistered pharmaceutical item in Hong Kong, adding that authorities would continue to investigate the case.
The department said it carried out an enforcement operation with police on Wednesday, arresting a 30-year-old woman in Mong Kok.
The enforcement action followed a South China Morning Post investigation in January that revealed online retailers and a local pharmacy were offering slimming injections without prescriptions.

The department urged the public not to purchase or use products of uncertain composition or from unknown sources.
“Purchasing controlled medicines, including slimming drugs, online poses health risks. Besides the lack of a doctor’s assessment of an individual’s health condition, it is difficult to ascertain the legitimate source of the drugs,” it said.
“It is also impossible to know whether the drugs were properly stored during transport especially for drugs requiring cold-chain storage. This leaves their safety, quality and efficacy unguaranteed.”
Under the ordinance, all pharmaceutical products must be registered before being legally sold in the market.
The department said selling medicines controlled under the ordinance illegally, regardless of the sales channel, carries criminal liability.
The maximum penalty for illegal sale or possession of unregistered pharmaceutical products or Part 1 poisons is a fine of HK$100,000 (US$13,000) and up to two years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Man arrested after 14 endangered reptiles found in Kwai Chung industrial site
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities arrested a man after the discovery of 14 endangered reptiles, including a crocodile and two pythons, at an industrial site in Kwai Chung.
A spokesman for the Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department said on Wednesday evening that officers arrested the man in a blitz operation the previous night.
“The AFCD will remain vigilant and continue to monitor and collect intelligence through various channels and proactively take enforcement action to combat offences involving endangered species,” the spokesman said.
The department took action on Tuesday following a complaint that some endangered animals were kept inside an industrial building in Kwai Chung.
During the operation, officers found a rock iguana, a crocodile, two pythons, three monitor lizards, and a Chinese water dragon.
Six turtles – a big-headed turtle, three Indochinese box turtles, a black-breasted leaf turtle and a keeled box turtle – were also found on the premises.
The man was intercepted on-site and arrested on suspicion of possessing endangered species listed under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora.
The 14 reptiles were seized for further investigation.
Under Hong Kong law, the maximum punishment for illegal possession of endangered species is a HK$10 million fine and 10 years’ imprisonment.

Hong Kong activists had called for the government to create a list of authorised pets, microchip turtles sold in the city and toughen penalties for letting pets loose in the wild after a carnivorous alligator snapping turtle was found eating fish in a New Territories pond last month.
An activist previously told the South China Morning Post that species such as snapping turtles, large lizards and predatory fish were fundamentally incompatible with urban environments.
But abandoned turtles that are unable to survive in the city have been found every month, with the size of specimens increasing.
Activists also urged authorities to amend the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance to cover animals beyond cats and dogs.

data.title: Hong Kong tycoons downplay impact of Middle East war on city’s fortunes
data.description:
Hong Kong tycoons have downplayed the impact of the escalating Middle East conflict on the city, while expressing confidence in the region’s economic outlook amid continued strikes by the United States and Israel on Iran and Tehran’s retaliatory attacks.
Speaking to the media in Beijing on the sidelines of the “two sessions” – the nation’s annual parliamentary meetings – on Wednesday, New World Development scion Adrian Cheng Chi-kong said the Middle East’s economic prospects remained promising.
“Most of the Middle East’s economies are mature; we are cautiously optimistic about their prospects,” the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) member said.
The former heir apparent to the Hong Kong property giant also said the city could benefit from turmoil.
“If Hong Kong continues as a superconnector between the mainland and the world while cementing its role as an international financial centre, it will emerge as an excellent safe haven for capital,” he said.

Cheng’s remarks came after US-Israel strikes on Iran disrupted global supply chains and drove up oil prices, as US President Donald Trump warned the war could last four to five weeks.
Hong Kong stocks fell to a nearly three-month low on Wednesday as surging oil prices fuelled bets that rising energy costs would stoke inflation and cripple global growth.
Fellow CPPCC member Francis Lui Yiu-tung also believed the conflict would have little impact on Hong Kong, saying he had not observed any major effect on the city’s property or stock markets.
“Of course, we always hope for geopolitical stability, which would benefit all aspects of Hong Kong,” said the chairman of the local conglomerate K. Wah Group. “The city has always been seen as very open and stable, so [the conflicts] will not have a huge impact on us.”

Clarence Ling Chun-kit, director of developer Kwai Hung Group and a CPPCC member, argued the Middle East’s instability actually underscored Hong Kong’s status as a safe haven for global capital.
He cited the city’s diversified yuan products and derivatives, which let investors hedge exchange rates, interest rates and credit risks rather than concentrate exposure in a single currency or market.
Ling said the government and private sector should focus resources towards building better platforms that connect mainland enterprises with global capital.
“When Middle Eastern capital seeks exposure to mainland China or the broader Asian market, Hong Kong provides convenient channels through its listing platform, and stock and Bond Connect schemes, meeting the needs of both ‘investing in China’ and ‘reaching into Asia’,” he said.
“Its common law framework, robust regulation and high liquidity also help investors better diversify geographical, currency and policy risks than many other markets in the region.”
But Ling conceded the conflict had dampened his and his friends’ interest in investing in the region, adding that he was no longer planning to lead a delegation there any time soon after weighing the risks.
Meanwhile, business leaders reaffirmed their support for the Northern Metropolis after the city’s annual budget allocated new funds for the megaproject, which aims to turn 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land near the border with mainland China into new engines for economic growth.
Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office, told local delegates on Tuesday that Hong Kong must speed up work on the metropolis.
Lui called on the government to outline how it would allocate the HK$150 billion (US$19.2 billion) transferred from the Exchange Fund for the Northern Metropolis, as well as the megaproject’s role within the Greater Bay Area.
“The foremost is to get a clear understanding of the specific plans for the Northern Metropolis, and then we can consider increasing investment,” Lui said.
“I believe more communication is needed, particularly on our positioning and how to invest in a way that the market will find acceptable.”

Adam Kwok Kai-fai, executive director of Sun Hung Kai Properties, said the group would continue investing in the Northern Metropolis, where it is already a major stakeholder.
Kwok’s proposal during the two sessions calls on the government to accelerate the development of the Northern Metropolis university town by encouraging top mainland universities to set up branch campuses or research institutes.
He is also advocating a “dual-counter” trading for stocks and direct yuan investments in Hong Kong initial public offerings to advance the currency’s internationalisation.

data.title: China poised to expand global access to AI despite rivalry with US: Wang Jian
data.description:
Wang Jian, a prominent Chinese artificial intelligence scholar and director at Zhejiang Lab, said China had both the “resources and responsibility” to make AI more accessible globally at a time when other countries, such as the US, were increasingly acting in their own interests.
Speaking to the South China Morning Post on Wednesday on the sidelines of China’s annual annual legislative “two sessions” meetings in Beijing, Wang said the world remained far from the goal of turning AI into an “international public product” available to all.
Chinese President Xi Jinping first coined the phrase in a message to the World Smart Industry Expo in Chongqing in September, 2025.
Wang, a member of the CPPCC, stressed the importance of ensuring that both nations and individuals could benefit from AI capabilities.
When asked whether China had a responsibility to pursue that goal, Wang said the country was not only “definitely responsible” but also “the only country in the world that can still contribute to global technological advancement while committing to its ideals”.
He added that at a time when nations were increasingly acting in their own interests – with the US a typical example – China had the “resources and responsibility” to make AI equally accessible to everyone.
Wang also said that China’s electricity supply and energy consumption structure could support the rapid growth of AI infrastructure, particularly compared with the US, where technology executives including Tesla founder and CEO Elon Musk had warned about electricity shortages for AI data centres.
He said parts of the US electricity infrastructure were outdated, while many manufacturers of equipment needed to build new power capacity were based in China, making it more difficult for the US to ramp up its energy supply.
Qi Xiangdong, chairman of cybersecurity firm Qi An Xin Technology Group and also a member of the CPPCC, said China’s competitive edge in AI lay in its vast troves of data and the breadth of industries where the technology could be deployed.
“We were the first to set up a national data administration, and we have centralised access to large volumes of data, which are the basic elements for advancing the AI sector,” Qi told media outlets including the SCMP on Wednesday.

Qi acknowledged that the US appeared to be ahead of China in terms of original AI innovation, but said China’s strength lay in applying the technology across a wide range of industries and real-world scenarios.
“The AI race is a race of a hundred years that has only just begun,” he said. “We don’t yet know who will be the winner in the end.”
Wang, meanwhile, returned to the theme of global collaboration, reiterating his call to expand research into space-based computing and artificial intelligence.
He pointed to the Three-Body Computing Constellation, a space-based computing infrastructure project led by Zhejiang Lab, and said his proposal to “put AI into space” had attracted growing international attention.
Wang, who founded the cloud computing division of Alibaba Group Holding, also referenced remarks he made at last year’s AI for Good Global Summit in Geneva, where he highlighted the potential role of AI and computing in humanity’s exploration of deep space. Alibaba owns the SCMP.
He noted that the AI and Space Computing Challenge, jointly launched by Zhejiang Lab and the United Nations’ International Telecommunication Union, had attracted more than 200 teams from about 45 countries.
“This is a new and imaginative technology, and it belongs to the world – so I was quite happy with the response,” Wang said. “Your technology is not persuasive if it is not being enjoyed by users across the globe.”

data.title: China’s EV battery leader CATL to step up use of AI in R&D: founder
data.description:
The founder and chairman of Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (CATL) said his company plans to ramp up the use of artificial intelligence in research and take Chinese battery technology and standards further overseas, as the battery giant maps the next phase of growth.
The world’s largest maker of electric vehicle (EV) batteries would step up research and development (R&D) spending and apply more AI methods to strengthen its technological edge, said billionaire Robin Zeng Yuqun at a briefing on Wednesday during China’s “two sessions” – the annual legislative meetings of the National Committee of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) and the National People’s Congress (NPC). Zeng is a member of the CPPCC.
His remarks came in response to a question about the rapid rise of China’s EVs, lithium-ion batteries and photovoltaic products – the so-called “new three” products that now account for nearly 1.3 trillion yuan (US$188 billion) in annual exports, helping to drive foreign trade growth.
Zeng highlighted the buildout of a comprehensive industrial ecosystem, stretching from upstream mining to materials, battery manufacturing, downstream applications and recycling. The strength of that supply chain, combined with a focus on indigenous innovation, had underpinned the sector’s competitiveness, he said.
“We have built a resilient supply chain and ecosystem in recent years,” he said. “Looking ahead, we will increase our R&D investment, especially by applying more AI methods.”
CATL would continue selling Chinese products to the world while promoting its technologies and standards globally, he added.
The company, which is based in Ningde, in east China’s Fujian province, has been expanding production bases overseas while deepening partnerships with international carmakers. It completed the largest initial public offering in Hong Kong in 2025, raising US$4.6 billion, with the proceeds earmarked to build factories in overseas markets, which account for 30 per cent of its total revenue.
Zeng’s ambition to move beyond manufacturing scale towards deeper integration of AI aligns with the aim of leading EV makers, which are also eyeing increased application of AI amid slowing growth.

Industry analysts expect government and industry officials to discuss plans to rein in domestic EV makers’ output while encouraging them to prioritise technological innovation during the two sessions meeting. Zeng and top bosses of China’s largest carmakers, including Geely Auto chairman Li Shufu and Chery Automobile chairman Yin Tongyue, will be there.
One focus would be improving automotive intelligence and autonomous driving, said Yale Zhang, managing director at the consultancy Automotive Foresight in Shanghai.
Geely, mainland China’s second-largest carmaker, said it would accelerate upgrades to its AI-enabled driver assistance system and roll it out across a broader range of models. Meanwhile, premium electric-vehicle maker Xpeng said it aimed to become the first company globally to mass-produce three cutting-edge AI-driven products – advanced humanoid robots, flying cars and robotaxis – this year.
He Xiaopeng, founder and CEO of Xpeng, said on Monday that China should skip an intermediate step and move directly to a more advanced version of autonomous driving, adding that Beijing should adjust regulations and policies faster to make this happen.

data.title: Air freight costs for China shippers may jump 30% as Middle East airspace closes
data.description:
Air freight costs for China-reliant shippers may rise by up to 30 per cent due to the suspension of Middle East air routes triggered by US and Israeli strikes on Iran, a Hong Kong-based logistics association said.
The Hong Kong Association of Freight Forwarding & Logistics made the estimate based on “market feedback” since the strikes began on Saturday, the trade group told the South China Morning Post in an emailed statement.
Most of the group’s 303 corporate members operate in mainland China.
Shippers moving cargo between China and Europe are facing longer flight times, increased congestion and higher fuel consumption, the statement said.
“As our chairman advised, the sudden suspension of Middle East air routes has already tightened global air‑freight capacity,” it said. “Many airlines have long relied on Middle Eastern airspace for Europe‑bound flights,” the statement added.
US and Israeli forces began missile strikes on Saturday after weeks of failed negotiations over Iran’s nuclear ambitions, triggering a conflict that US President Donald Trump said could last from several weeks to more than a month.
Air cargo capacity on Asia-Middle East-Europe routes fell 26 per cent over the weekend compared with the previous weekend because of airspace closures, logistics news website The Loadstar reported, citing consultancy Aevean.

Flights between Europe and East Asia that usually passed over Iran and Persian Gulf countries could reroute through a safer northern corridor over Azerbaijan and Georgia, causing a glut of aircraft along that path and “exceeding local air traffic control capacity”, independent aviation analyst Li Hanming said.
Some cargo flights had shifted to more stable routes, Li said, benefiting mainland Chinese airlines. Chinese carriers, unlike many Western peers, already fly through Russian airspace on routes to much of Europe.
Iran’s threats against ships passing near its coastline through the Strait of Hormuz have effectively closed the waterway, where about 10 per cent of the global container fleet normally passes.
The Hong Kong association said on Tuesday it was still evaluating the war’s impact on ocean freight, adding that its assessment would depend on “how long the disruption continues”.
“The association expresses grave concern over the potential impact that the closure of the Strait of Hormuz may have on global transport flows, which could in turn affect the freight and logistics sector,” the statement said.

data.title: More Hongkongers leave money in MPF pension plan amid market rally
data.description:
Fewer Hongkongers withdrew funds from the city’s mandatory pension plan in the fourth quarter of last year, as a stock market rally prompted more members to leave their investments in place even when they stopped working or left the city, according to analysts.
The number of people taking their money out of the Mandatory Provident Fund (MPF) because they were leaving Hong Kong permanently fell 39 per cent in the final three months of 2025 from the previous quarter, to 4,200 cases, according to data released by the pension regulator, the Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Authority (MPFA), on Wednesday.
The total amount withdrawn also declined by 38 per cent to HK$1.02 billion (US$130 million) from a quarter earlier, the MPFA data showed.
Withdrawals due to retirement or early retirement also declined. A total of 41,500 members withdrew their MPF balance for retirement reasons during the quarter, down 5 per cent from the third quarter. The sum these members withdrew declined 4 per cent to HK$6.6 billion, the authority’s data showed.
Withdrawals due to early retirement dropped by 2 per cent to 7,900, with the total amount withdrawn remaining almost unchanged at HK$2 billion.
The MPF has 4.8 million members.
“For retirees or early retired persons who have no urgent needs to withdraw their MPF immediately to support their living, they may consider keeping part or all of their retirement funds in the MPF to enjoy the investment returns from the market rally,” said Kenrick Chung, chief corporate solutions officer at Bay Insurance Brokers in Hong Kong.
Established in 2000, the MPF is a compulsory retirement scheme. It collects monthly contributions from employers and employees, equal to 5 per cent of a worker’s monthly salary from each, capped at a total of HK$3,000 a month. Employees can choose to invest their contributions in different funds and can make additional contributions.
Employees can apply to withdraw their contributions plus investment returns at age 65, or earlier if they choose to retire or leave the city permanently. Members can also opt to leave their money in the MPF or withdraw only part of it.
Strong MPF performance last year may have led some members to let their investments ride, according to Chung.
In the first two months of this year, strong investment gains and contributions brought total MPF assets to an all-time high of HK$1.63 trillion, an average of HK$340,216 per member, according to data from MPF Ratings, an independent consultant that tracks the programme’s performance.
That growth came after the MPF’s 378 investment funds racked up a total investment gain of HK$71.1 billion in the first two months of the year, after gaining HK$260 billion in 2025 as a whole, MPF Ratings’ data showed.
The MPF investment funds reported an average net return of 16.5 per cent in 2025 – the third consecutive year the scheme returned an annual gain, following an 8.6 per cent average net return in 2024 and 3.4 per cent in 2023.
However, Francis Chung, chairman of MPF Ratings, warned MPF members to beware of market volatility this year.
With no immediate signs of an end to the US-Iran war, investors have started to factor in the consequences of a prolonged conflict in the Middle East by trimming their exposure to risk assets. Higher oil prices will weigh on stocks and other risk assets by fanning inflation and force the US Federal Reserve to delay interest-rate cuts.

Hong Kong stocks fell to a nearly three-month low on Wednesday, as surging oil prices fuelled bets that rising energy costs would stoke inflation and cripple global growth. The Hang Seng Index closed 2 per cent lower at 25,249.48, the lowest since December 16.
Other key markets in the region also fell. Japan’s Nikkei 225 slumped 3.6 per cent, South Korea’s Kospi tumbled 12 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 sank 1.9 per cent.
“The world is presently highly unpredictable,” Chung said on Wednesday. “Diversification and long-term investing minimise uncertainty.”
He added that the MPFA’s mandated default investment strategy funds continued to be MPF Ratings’ preferred investment option for the programme’s members. The default investment strategy funds feature a diversified investment mix and automatically shift risk levels according to the member’s age.

data.title: Middle East exhibitors at Hong Kong jewellery fair rue poorer sales, attendance
data.description:
Middle Eastern exhibitors at the Hong Kong International Jewellery Show expect poorer business due to disruptions brought on by the US-Israel war against Iran, while organisers have reported “a small number” of participants being affected by flight cancellations and delays.
The event, which opened on Wednesday at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre, is being held alongside the Hong Kong International Diamond, Gem & Pearl Show at AsiaWorld-Expo. The two shows feature about 4,000 exhibitors from over 40 countries and regions.
The shows were launched just days after US-Israel strikes on Iran killed supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, his wife and other senior officials, and prompted retaliatory attacks across the Middle East.
Among the exhibitors at the jewellery show was Paritosh Doshi, chief operating officer of United Arab Emirates-based wholesaler Lumex, who predicted that sales for his business at the show would drop by at least 25 per cent.
“I am sure there will be fewer people visiting because Israel is shut too, so none of the Israelis will come in either,” he said. “None of the people from the United Arab Emirates and around it will be here, so I think the show will be slow because of that.”
Doshi explained that he and his wife had to fly to Hong Kong from India, where they are based, to attend the event, as the firm’s original plan to have a team from the UAE run its booth had fallen apart due to flight disruptions caused by the war.
Beyond the show, he added that Lumex’s imports into Dubai and its shipments to clients had also been placed on hold.
Doshi said he was monitoring the situation but it was still too early to predict how the conflict would affect the company’s longer-term prospects.

In a reply to the South China Morning Post, show organiser Hong Kong Trade Development Council said that the travel plans of “a small number of exhibitors and buyers” had been affected by flight cancellations and delays.
“We have activated contingency measures, including immediate on-site assistance to exhibitors in displaying promotional information,” a spokesman said.
Other exhibitors also expressed concern that the war would lead to a dip in business at the show.
S. Vinodkumar Diamonds, whose UAE branch is exhibiting at the show, said it expected a drop in business as Middle Eastern customers might not be able to visit the city due to flight cancellations.
Aashay Shah, head of marketing at the firm’s Indian parent company, added that the decrease was not limited to customers in the region, as clients from Europe may also decide not to visit the city to avoid getting caught up in disruptions.
“People from Europe are scared to travel,” he said. “What if they get stuck in another country or cannot go back to their country? Anything can happen.”

data.title: China biotech outlicensing tops US$52 billion in first 2 months
data.description:
The pace and size of outlicensing deals between Chinese drug makers and global partners have accelerated this year, highlighted by two agreements announced this week, each worth more than US$1 billion.
The string of deals shows how China is becoming a sought-after source of drug candidates for multinational pharmaceutical companies, which are increasingly looking for long-term partnerships rather than one-off arrangements, analysts said.
The latest deals, Sino Biopharmaceutical’s agreement with France’s Sanofi worth up to US$1.53 billion and Antengene Corporation’s agreement with Belgium’s UCB valued at about US$1.18 billion, came as cross-border transactions by Chinese drug makers in the first two months of the year outpaced any single quarter last year in upfront payments and total value, according to Yang Huang, head of China healthcare research at JPMorgan Chase.
Total 2026 deal value from China-originated outlicensing activities reached US$52 billion from 41 transactions as of February 24, following a record 157 deals worth a combined US$135.7 billion last year.

Against this commercial backdrop, markets are watching for signals of government support for innovative drug development as China’s “two sessions” begin on Wednesday, although industry analysts largely expect only limited “moral support”.
Hong Kong-listed Sino Biopharmaceutical said in an exchange filing on Wednesday that its Chia Tai Tianqing Pharmaceutical unit had granted a subsidiary of Sanofi the exclusive worldwide rights to develop, manufacture and sell rovadicitinib, an oral drug to treat blood cancer and immune-related diseases.
Under the agreement, Sino Biopharmaceutical will receive an upfront payment of US$135 million plus potential development, regulatory and sales milestone payments of up to US$1.39 billion, as well as up to double-digit tiered royalties based on the annual net sales of rovadicitinib.
The drug was approved by China’s National Medical Products Administration last month for the treatment of a type of blood cancer.
On Tuesday, Antengene granted Brussels-listed UCB an exclusive worldwide licence to manufacture and commercialise ATG-201, an experimental antibody-based therapy treating autoimmune diseases, according to an exchange filing.
Antengene, which has a research and development centre and manufacturing sites in China, will receive upfront and near-term milestone payments of US$80 million and US$1.1 billion, respectively, as well as tiered royalties on future net sales.
“One important structural shift is that global multinational corporations are no longer just buying cheap molecules, but they are paying premiums to secure Chinese platforms and sustained codevelopment,” Huang said.
Other notable outlicensing deals so far this year include a January 29 tie-up in which AstraZeneca agreed to pay US$1.2 billion upfront to China’s CSPC in a weight-loss drug collaboration worth up to US$18.5 billion, and an early-February Innovent-Lilly partnership with a US$350 million upfront payment and as much as US$8.5 billion in milestones for next-generation cancer and immunology drugs.
“Those deals highlight a new collaboration paradigm in which global companies secure long-term access to Chinese innovation platforms while China partners lead early discovery and regional clinical execution,” Huang said.
Tony Ren, head of Asia healthcare research at Macquarie Capital, said 2026 would “probably see another 50 to 60 per cent increase in deal value”, after more than doubling in 2025.
“Every large pharmaceutical company is sending its business development and research and development teams to China to scout for new drug assets,” Ren said.

data.title: Hong Kong launches 3-year action plan to help residents fight the flab
data.description:
Hong Kong has launched its first action plan on weight management, aiming to reduce the proportion of people who are overweight or obese to less than half the population in three years from the current 51.3 per cent of adults.
On Wednesday, World Obesity Day, the Department of Health announced more than 40 measures, ranging from installing more weighing scales in government venues to exploring the inclusion of novel weight-loss injections in the drug formulary, in a bid to reverse the rising trend of obesity in the city.
The proportion of people who are obese or overweight has been rising over the past decades, from 37.2 per cent in 2004, to 47 per cent in 2014 and 51.3 per cent in 2022.
A study in 2022 found that more than two-thirds of people who were overweight did not know they were, with 40 per cent having taken no action to manage their weight in the previous 12 months.
Edwin Tsui Lok-kin, controller of the Centre for Health Protection, said the focus of the first year was raising awareness about obesity, a major risk factor for chronic illnesses and 13 types of cancer. Awareness was the starting point of intervention, he said.
“We hope to truly change the public’s perception of weight management, and provide support accordingly through different stakeholders in the community and different government departments,” Tsui said.
“Weight management is not blindly losing weight, but finding a balance in diet and exercise. Walking a few more steps or running to catch the bus are also forms of exercise.”

The focus for the second and third years will be on pushing for positive changes in lifestyle and social environment, and turning weight management into a part of people’s lives.
Tsui stressed that the fight against obesity was not a personal choice or discipline but a multidimensional public health challenge that involved environmental, social and economic factors, warranting cross-departmental collective efforts.
The action plan not only involved health authorities, but also the Education Bureau, Leisure and Cultural Services Department, Environment and Ecology Bureau, and Planning Department, among others, he added.
Dr Anne Chee, head of the centre’s non-communicable disease branch, said the authorities hoped the action plan would help to bring down the 51.3 per cent prevalence of overweight and obesity to below 50 per cent.
“If using the same scale [as the World Health Organization], the Hong Kong rate is 40 per cent, which is slightly better than the 43 per cent average of WHO member states,” she said.
A highlight of the action plan is to foster a social environment that supports weight management by providing more scales and measuring tapes in sports centres and schools. Companies that sign a charter to promote a healthy workplace will receive a free weighing scale.
The Planning Department and Housing Department would also adopt more “active design”, such as installing more fitness equipment in public housing estates and building more cycling tracks and jogging trails, Tsui said.
To promote a healthy diet, the Environment and Ecology Bureau and the Food and Environmental Hygiene Department will strengthen public education on understanding food labels, and encourage the food trade to reduce the sodium and sugar content in products.


The government will also strengthen health service delivery, enhancing the role of district health centres as the “gatekeeper” of weight management.
That means the centres will conduct health assessments and their cross-disciplinary teams of nurses, dietitians and therapists will provide support.
People who have developed chronic conditions will be referred to family doctors in their network or Hospital Authority specialists.
The government will also explore including novel weight-loss drugs, such as injectable GLP-1, in the drug formulary with reference to WHO guidelines.
To better monitor residents’ weight, the government will, with consent from account holders, collect and analyse big data from eHealth, the electronic health record platform.
Asian adults with a body mass index (BMI) between 23 and less than 25 are considered overweight, according to the WHO’s Western Pacific region. Those with a BMI of 25 or above are considered obese.

data.title: Hong Kong’s retail sales rise 5.5% in January as tourists loosen purse strings
data.description:
Hong Kong’s retail sales rose 5.5 per cent year on year in January, with authorities crediting inbound tourism for supporting the sector.
Provisional figures released by the Census and Statistics Department on Wednesday showed sales reached HK$37.3 billion (US$4.77 billion) in January, marking the ninth straight month of growth since May 2025, after 14 consecutive months of decline.
The increase ended a four-month streak of growth above 6 per cent, but the government cautioned that the slower pace was partly due to the Chinese New Year holiday – traditionally a peak sales season – falling in February compared with January last year.
A government spokesman said the retail sector’s recovery remained solid despite last January’s high base from an early Chinese New Year.
He added that total retail sales rose significantly in January compared with the previous month on a seasonally adjusted basis.
“Looking ahead, robust economic growth and sustained visitor arrivals will continue to support local consumption and benefit retailers,” he said.
Annie Tse Yau On-yee, chairwoman of the Hong Kong Retail Management Association, said the figures reflected that growth in the sector had been sustainable.
“The overall retail sales in January have been satisfactory. Although we are still falling short of the historical peak, the upwards trend since September last year has been sustainable,” she said.
Sales of electrical goods and other consumer durables hit HK$4 billion in January, surging 38.7 per cent.
Sales of jewellery, watches, clocks and valuable gifts rose 31.1 per cent, while motor vehicles and parts climbed 18.5 per cent.

Tse, who is also the chairwoman and CEO of Tse Sui Luen Jewellery (International) Limited, said the number of items sold in the jewellery sector had actually dropped over the period, but the soaring gold price had returned an increase in sales value.
Retail sales of wearing apparel surged 5.7 per cent year on year, while fuel sales fell 17.5 per cent to HK$627 million.
In January, the city recorded 4.81 million visitor arrivals, up 1 per cent compared with a year ago, with 76 per cent coming from mainland China. Hong Kong is expected to receive 53.8 million visitors this year, an 8 per cent increase over 2025.
The association also polled its members, who operate over 8,000 outlets in the city, on their performance over the Chinese New Year holiday, and found more than 60 per cent of them recorded better sales than the same period last year.
Tse said the industry was cautiously optimistic about the prospects. She cited growth drivers such as the improved business environment and booming inbound tourism that were weighed against uncertainties brought by military conflicts in the Middle East, residents’ cross-border spending and a bearish stock market.
Additional reporting by Oscar Liu
